relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Strength exists to bear weakness, not to please itself.
Neighbor-good and building up guide freedom.
Messiah did not live for self-pleasing.
Scripture gives endurance, encouragement, and hope.
The Divine of endurance shapes one-mindedness in Messiah.
One accord and one mouth glorify the Divine.
Receive one another as Messiah received you.
Messiah serves circumcision to confirm ancestral promises.
The nations glorify the Divine for mercy.
Nations are invited to rejoice with his people.
All nations and peoples are summoned to praise.
The root of Jesse becomes hope for nations.
The Divine of hope fills trust with joy, peace, and Spirit-power.
The siblings are full of goodness and able to admonish.
Paul writes boldly as reminder by grace.
His service priestly-offers the nations, sanctified in holy Spirit.
His boasting is in Messiah toward the Divine.
Only what Messiah worked through him is claimed.
Signs, wonders, and Spirit-power fulfill the good news arc.
He longs to announce where Messiah has not been named.
The unheard will see and understand.
This mission has often delayed Rome.
The longing to come remains after the regional work.
Spain and Rome become future fellowship and sending.
First, Paul goes to Jerusalem to serve the holy ones.
Macedonia and Achaia share with Jerusalem’s poor.
Spiritual sharing creates material reciprocity.
The fruit of sharing is sealed before Spain.
Paul expects fullness of Messiah’s blessing.
He asks for prayer-struggle through Messiah and Spirit-love.
He seeks rescue and acceptable service in Jerusalem.
He hopes to come in joy and rest together.
The Divine of peace is invoked over all.

contemplative_note: Romans 15 is reciprocal mission: nations praise with Israel, the strong bear the not-strong, and spiritual blessing returns as material service.
